content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

July 27, 1977

77-07-099

Honorable Joseph W. Chandler
Santa Monica Municipal Court
1725 Main Street
Santa Monica, CA 90401

Dear Judge Chandler:

On February 25, 1977, I wrote to you in response
to your request for advice and stated that under certain cir-
cumstances the use of a bank credit card (for example, Bank-
americard or Master Charge) or a credit card issued by a com-
mercial establishment could create a reporting obligation for
a public official such as yourself. In particular, I indi-
cated that if a bill were not paid during the normal billing
cycle and the amount overdue exceeded $250, the credit card
holder would have received a reportable loan within the mean-
ing of Government Code Section 82010. I further stated that
the staff was dissatisfied with this conclusion but thought
that the statute dictated the result.

Recently, the staff, in response to another advice
letter, decided to reverse its earlier position. It concluded
that despite the fact that a literal reading of the
definition of the term "income" suggests that an extension
of credit through the use of a credit card would create a
reporting obligation, the policy objectives of the Act are
not best served by such an interpretation. Under the new
interpretation a filer will incur a reporting obligation by
reason of an extension of credit "only if the credit is ex-
tended on special terms not available to similarly situated
members of the general public." Accordingly, since I assume
you are entitled to an extension of credit on the same terms
as other individuals who hold the types of credit cards about
which you are concerned, you will not incur a reporting obli-
gation even when you do not pay a bill within the normal billing
cycle.

244

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE LEGAL 322-5901 • ENFORCEMENT CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-0441

Honorable Joseph W. Chandler
Page two
July 27, 1977


I hope the reversal of our earlier advice does not
inconvenience you. If you have any questions, please contact
Michael Baker, the Chief of the Commission's Legal Division.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Kenneth H. Finney
Staff Counsel
Legal Division

KHF:jo

February 25, 1977


Honorable Joseph W. Chandler
Santa Monica Municipal Court
1725 Main Street
Santa Monica, CA 90401

Dear Judge Chandler:

     This letter will serve to confirm our telephone con-
versation of February 23, 1977, in which I provided the an-
swers to the questions posed in your letter of February 7, 1977.
Your first question involved potential reporting obligations
based on the use of a bank credit card such as Bankamericard
or Master Charge. I informed you that the staff has determined
that if a bank credit card bill is paid within the normal bill-
ing cycle (in other words, no interest charge is incurred),
the use of the card would not create a reporting obligation
under the Political Reform Act. On the other hand, I also
stated that if an interest charge is incurred and the amount
of the bill exceeds $250, the filer will have received a loan
within the meaning of Government Code Section 82030 which is
reportable. I further indicated to you that the staff was
not satisfied with this conclusion but felt constrained by the
statute to reach it. However, in light of the staff's dissatis-
faction, I think that a regulation or a legislative amendment
to the Act will be proposed in order to rectify the situation.

     Your second question was similar to the first but
involved the use of a credit card issued by a commercial es-
tablishment. I responded that our answer is the same. Ac-
cordingly, if the bill is paid within the normal billing cycle,
no reporting obligation will result. If, however, an interest
charge is incurred and the outstanding balance exceeds $250,
this will constitute a reportable loan from the commercial
establishment.

     Your third question inquired about fees that you re-
ceive in exchange for performing wedding ceremonies. I re-
sponded that such fees are income if the amount of the charge
is the usual fee charged by a judge for performing this service,

Honorable Joseph W. Chandler
Page Two
February 25, 1977


and that a gift will be involved only if the fee received were
extraordinarily high. In particular, I indicated that fees
of $50 to $75 would be income and not gifts. I also pointed
out that it is not necessary to cumulate all fees received from
performing wedding ceremonies to determine if the $250 report-
ing threshold is met.

Your fourth question inquired about payments you
receive from the Los Angeles County Employees' Retirement
Board and payments that you make to the Judges' State Retire-
ment Fund. I indicated that the payments you receive from the
Los Angeles County Employees' Retirement Board are not report-
able as income because they are "salary...received from a...
local government agency" within the meaning of Government Code
Section 82030(b)(2). This conclusion is based on an opinion
of the Commission which will be adopted at the March meeting
but already has been approved in principle. With respect to
the payments you make to the Judges' State Retirement Fund,
these do not involve an investment because the Retirement Fund
is not a "business entity."

Your fifth question concerned income tax refunds from
the federal and state governments. I indicated that tax re-
funds are not income within the meaning of Government Code Sec-
tion 82030.

Finally, you inquired whether interest paid to you
by the federal government because of a delay in affecting your
tax refund would be income. I responded that this type of
payment would be income and would be reportable if it amounted
to $250 or more.

I believe the foregoing accurately reflects the sub-
stance of our telephone conversation. If you have any further
questions, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Sincerely,


Kenneth Finney
Chief
Legal Division

KF:jo

sections.question: s, please do not hesitate to contact me.
sections.conclusion: Under the new interpretation, a filer will incur a reporting obligation by reason of an extension of credit "only if the credit is extended on special terms not available to similarly situated members of the general public." Therefore, if the credit is on the same terms available to the general public, no reporting obligation exists. Additionally: wedding ceremony fees of $50-$75 are income (not gifts); retirement board payments are not reportable as "salary" from a local government agency; retirement fund contributions are not investments; tax refunds are not reportable income; and interest paid by the federal government on delayed tax refunds is reportable income if it exceeds $250.
sections.question_synthetic: Judge Chandler seeks clarification on multiple income reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act, including credit card extensions, wedding ceremony fees, retirement fund payments, and tax refunds.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC reversed its earlier position on credit card reporting obligations, determining that extensions of credit do not create reporting obligations unless extended on special terms not available to the general public. Judge Chandler's other inquiries regarding wedding fees, retirement payments, and tax refunds were addressed with specific guidance on reportability thresholds.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document contains two letters dated July 27, 1977 and February 25, 1977. The July 27 letter is the primary response indicating a reversal of position, while the February 25 letter contains the original detailed advice. OCR quality affects some character recognition but key content is discernible. The synthetic conclusion draws from both letters to provide comprehensive guidance.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: s, please do not hesitate to contact me.

CONCLUSION: Under the new interpretation, a filer will incur a reporting obligation by reason of an extension of credit "only if the credit is extended on special terms not available to similarly situated members of the general public." Therefore, if the credit is on the same terms available to the general public, no reporting obligation exists. Additionally: wedding ceremony fees of $50-$75 are income (not gifts); retirement board payments are not reportable as "salary" from a local government agency; retirement fund contributions are not investments; tax refunds are not reportable income; and interest paid by the federal government on delayed tax refunds is reportable income if it exceeds $250.

embedding.summary: This advice letter responds to multiple questions from Santa Monica Municipal Court Judge Joseph W. Chandler regarding reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act, with particular focus on a reversal of the FPPC's prior position on credit card extensions and clarification on various types of income and payments.
